---
title: "Pennsylvania Blackberry (Rubus pensilvanicus)"
slug: "rudbeckia-hirta"
group: "Plantae"
image: "https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/180778959/medium.jpeg"        # keep for thumbnails
image-alt: "Pennsylvania Blackberry"  # keep for a11y
tax_kingdom: "Plantae"
tax_class: "Magnoliopsida"
tax_order: "Rosales"
tax_family: "Rosaceae"
categories: ["Plantae", "Magnoliopsida", "Rosales", "Rosaceae"]   # these drive the chips + filtering
freeze: true
---

<figure class="species-figure">

<img src="https://inaturalist-open-data.s3.amazonaws.com/photos/180778959/medium.jpeg" alt="Pennsylvania Blackberry" class="species-photo"/>

<figcaption class="photo-credit">

Photo: <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/people/lyngettys">
lyngettys</a> (CC BY-NC) via <a href="https://www.inaturalist.org/observations/107480410">iNaturalist</a>

</figcaption>

</figure>

## Taxonomy

-   **Class:** Magnoliopsida
-   **Order:** Rosales
-   **Family:** Rosaceae

## Occurrence at DeLuca Preserve

-   [DeLuca Bio Blitz Species Observations](https://www.inaturalist.org/observations?project_id=191727&subview=map&taxon_id=78894)

## Conservation & rarity context

-   IUCN: Not Listed
-   **Rarity note:** Rare species in Florida found in fields, forest edges, and disturbed areas.

## Natural history notes

A thorny shrub characterized by its arching canes, compound leaves, and clusters of white flowers followed by black, juicy berries. In Florida, it is found in a variety of habitats including fields, forest edges, and disturbed areas. It grows during the warmer months, and its fruits provide an important food source for birds and other wildlife.

## References / links

-   [iNaturalist species page](https://www.inaturalist.org/taxa/78894-Rubus-pensilvanicus)
-   [Local profile](https://florida.plantatlas.usf.edu/plant/species/2256)
